subject: Criminal Law
lesson_title: DVA 2018, Coercive Control, and the Coco Act

questions.question: The Domestic Violence Act 2018 commenced on:
questions.explanation: The Domestic Violence Act 2018 commenced on 2 January 2019. It repealed and replaced the Domestic Violence Acts 1996 to 2011 and introduced a modern comprehensive framework with a civil orders regime and criminal sentencing aggravation.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four civil orders available under the Domestic Violence Act 2018?
questions.explanation: The four civil orders under the DVA 2018 are: safety order, barring order, emergency barring order, and protection order. An 'exclusion order' is not a term used in the 2018 Act — this is a common distractor. The protection order is a temporary measure pending a full hearing.

questions.question: Under the Domestic Violence Act 2018, a safety order can be granted for a maximum period of:
questions.explanation: A safety order under the DVA 2018 can be granted for up to five years. It prohibits the respondent from engaging in violent or threatening behaviour but does not exclude them from the home — unlike a barring order.

questions.question: The offence of coercive control in Ireland is created by:

options.A: Section 10 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997
options.B: Section 2 of the Harassment, Harmful Communications and Related Offences Act 2020
options.C: Section 39 of the Domestic Violence Act 2018
options.D: Section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act 1964

questions.explanation: The offence of coercive control is created by Section 39 of the Domestic Violence Act 2018. It was the first time psychological and emotional abuse in intimate relationships was made a criminal offence in Ireland. The maximum penalty is five years imprisonment on indictment.

questions.question: For a conviction under Section 39 DVA 2018, the behaviour of the accused must satisfy four requirements. Which of the following is NOT one of them?

options.A: The behaviour must be controlling or coercive in nature
options.C: The accused and victim must be in or have been in an intimate relationship
options.D: The accused must have previously been convicted of a domestic violence offence

questions.explanation: A prior conviction is not a requirement for Section 39. The four requirements are: (1) the behaviour is controlling or coercive; (2) it is persistent — a pattern rather than a single act; (3) the parties are or were in an intimate relationship; and (4) the behaviour has a serious effect on the victim's day-to-day activities or causes serious alarm or distress.

questions.question: In DPP v Dunleavy [2020] Circuit Court, the accused was convicted of coercive control for:

options.A: Physically assaulting his partner on repeated occasions over a six-month period
options.B: Making over six thousand phone calls to his partner over a three-month period
options.C: Distributing intimate images of his partner without consent
options.D: Threatening to kill his partner unless she withdrew a barring order application

questions.explanation: In DPP v Dunleavy [2020] Circuit Court — one of the first prosecutions under Section 39 — the accused made over six thousand phone calls to his partner over three months. The court held this relentless contact as a tool of domination constituted coercive control. He received a three-year sentence, partially suspended.

questions.question: DPP v Jake Boles [2023] IECA is significant in the context of coercive control sentencing because:

options.A: It was the first case in which coercive control was charged alongside murder
options.B: The court applied the Mahoney principle to set a sentencing benchmark where few precedents existed
options.C: It held that coercive control is a lesser offence than assault and should carry a suspended sentence
options.D: It introduced the concept of a 'control diary' as evidence of coercive conduct

questions.explanation: In DPP v Jake Boles [2023] IECA, Edwards J applied the Mahoney principle — where there are no prior comparable cases, the court must set a benchmark itself. The court noted the five-year maximum and stressed coercive control warrants real custodial sentences. Boles is a norm-setting sentencing judgment under Section 39.

questions.question: The Harassment, Harmful Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 — the Coco Act — commenced on:
questions.explanation: The Harassment, Harmful Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 — the Coco Act — commenced on 9 February 2021. It modernised Ireland's legal response to technology-facilitated abuse, including non-consensual distribution of intimate images.

questions.question: Under Section 2 of the Coco Act 2020, the maximum penalty for non-consensual distribution of intimate images is:
questions.explanation: Section 2 of the Coco Act carries a maximum of seven years imprisonment on indictment for non-consensual distribution of intimate images. Section 4 (grossly offensive communications) carries a lower maximum of two years. Candidates must know the correct penalties for both provisions.

questions.question: The Coco Act 2020 amended Section 10 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 specifically to address which gap in the harassment offence?

options.A: It extended the offence to cover workplace harassment
options.B: It extended the offence to cover indirect harassment conducted through third parties
options.C: It reduced the maximum penalty for online harassment to two years
options.D: It created a new civil remedy for harassment victims

questions.explanation: The Coco Act amended Section 10 NFOATP 1997 to extend the harassment offence to indirect harassment — harassment conducted through third parties rather than directly to the victim. Before this amendment, a harasser who communicated with the victim through intermediaries could argue they had not harassed the victim directly. That gap is now closed.
